findings.title: All ETH, extracted profits, and arbitrary ERC20s are permanently locked in the contract

best_agent_match.title: Hardcoded external addresses are used without chain or code validation

findings.title: Forced ETH donations can permanently brick `executeOnOpportunity`

best_agent_match.title: Counter state is fully permissionless and can be arbitrarily rewritten by any caller

findings.title: Anyone can permissionlessly trigger the hardcoded exploit once the contract is funded

best_agent_match.title: The exploit path never verifies that the token-side corruption actually happened before swapping

findings.title: A successful run can self-brick all future executions by ratcheting the profit baseline with trapped proceeds

best_agent_match.title: The exploit path never verifies that the token-side corruption actually happened before swapping

findings.title: Prefunded WETH can spoof the profitability check

best_agent_match.title: Counter state is fully permissionless and can be arbitrarily rewritten by any caller

rejected_candidates.title: Counter state is fully permissionless and can be arbitrarily rewritten by any caller
rejected_candidates.reason: `Counter.sol` is a trivial sample contract whose only state is intentionally public and mutable; no realistic protocol-level harm is evidenced from this codebase.

rejected_candidates.title: Hardcoded external addresses are used without chain or code validation
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a deployment/configuration assumption in a purpose-built verifier, not an exploitable in-protocol vulnerability; misdeploying to the wrong chain is operator error rather than a reportable bug.

rejected_candidates.title: The exploit path never verifies that the token-side corruption actually happened before swapping
rejected_candidates.reason: If the assumed token bug does not occur, the downstream Uniswap swap reverts and the transaction rolls back; the code does not create a distinct, realistic loss or lockup beyond already-reported liveness issues.
